source_intake_record.raw_question_text: At low temperatures, a mixture of ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ and ${ }^{4} \text{He}$ atoms form a liquid which separates into two phases: a concentrated phase (nearly pure ${ }^{3} \text{He}$), and a dilute phase (roughly $6.5 \text{\textperthousand} { }^{3} \text{He}$ for $T \text{\textless}= 0.1 \text{ K}$). The lighter ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ floats on top of the dilute phase, and ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ atoms can cross the phase boundary (see Fig. 2.23). The superfluid ${ }^{4} \text{He}$ has negligible excitation, and the thermodynamics of the dilute phase can be represented as an ideal degenerate Fermi gas of particles with density $n_{d}$ and effective mass $m^{*}\text{(}m^{*}\text{ is larger than }m_{3}\text{, the mass of the bare }{ }^{3} \text{He atom, due to the presence of the liquid }{ }^{4} \text{He, actually }m^{*}=2.4 m_{3}\text{)}$. We can crudely represent the concentrated phase by an ideal degenerate Fermi gas of density $n_{c}$ and particle mass $m_{3}$. Suppose the container in the figure is now connected to external plumbing so that ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ atoms can be transferred from the concentrated phase to the dilute phase at a rate of $N_{s}$ atoms per second (as in a dilution refrigerator). For fixed temperature $T$, how much power can this system absorb?

metadata.reasoning_chain:
The entropy per particle at low temperature is
\[
S(T)=\int_{0}^{T}  rac{c_{v}}{T} d T=\lambda  rac{T}{T_{\mathrm{F}}}, \quad 	ext { where } \lambda 	ext { is a constant. }
\]

The power absorbed is converted to latent heat, being
\[
W=\dot{N}_{s}\left(S_{\mathrm{d}}(T)-S_{\mathrm{c}}(T)ight) T=\dot{N}_{s} T^{2}\left( rac{\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}}{T_{\mathrm{Fd}}}- rac{\lambda_{\mathrm{c}}}{T_{\mathrm{Fc}}}ight) .
\]

normalized_value.title: SeePhys 开放题
normalized_value.rewritten_question_text: At low temperatures, a mixture of ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ and ${ }^{4} \text{He}$ atoms form a liquid which separates into two phases: a concentrated phase (nearly pure ${ }^{3} \text{He}$), and a dilute phase (roughly $6.5 \text{\textperthousand} { }^{3} \text{He}$ for $T \text{\textless}= 0.1 \text{ K}$). The lighter ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ floats on top of the dilute phase, and ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ atoms can cross the phase boundary (see Fig. 2.23). The superfluid ${ }^{4} \text{He}$ has negligible excitation, and the thermodynamics of the dilute phase can be represented as an ideal degenerate Fermi gas of particles with density $n_{d}$ and effective mass $m^{*}\text{(}m^{*}\text{ is larger than }m_{3}\text{, the mass of the bare }{ }^{3} \text{He atom, due to the presence of the liquid }{ }^{4} \text{He, actually }m^{*}=2.4 m_{3}\text{)}$. We can crudely represent the concentrated phase by an ideal degenerate Fermi gas of density $n_{c}$ and particle mass $m_{3}$. Suppose the container in the figure is now connected to external plumbing so that ${ }^{3} \text{He}$ atoms can be transferred from the concentrated phase to the dilute phase at a rate of $N_{s}$ atoms per second (as in a dilution refrigerator). For fixed temperature $T$, how much power can this system absorb?
